mnemonics.title: PEAKS = Five Ingredients
mnemonics.content: Plaintext, Encryption Algorithm, A secret Key, ciphertext (Scrambled output), decryption algorithm (Same key). PEAKS summarizes the five components of the symmetric cipher model.

mnemonics.title: SAME KEY = Symmetric
mnemonics.content: Symmetric = SAME key for both encryption and decryption. Asymmetric = DIFFERENT keys (public/private pair). If both sides share one key, it is symmetric.

mnemonics.title: Kerckhoffs: Key, Not Algorithm
mnemonics.content: Security depends on KEY secrecy, NOT algorithm secrecy. The algorithm can be public. Remember: 'Kerckhoffs says Keep the Key secret, not the Kode (algorithm).'

mnemonics.title: CKCP Attack Ladder
mnemonics.content: Ciphertext-Only < Known-Plaintext < Chosen-Plaintext < Chosen-Ciphertext. Each level gives the attacker more power. CKCP = Cats Know Clever Puzzles.

mnemonics.title: SubTrans = Substitution + Transposition
mnemonics.content: Two basic encryption operations: SUBstitution (replace letters) + TRANSposition (rearrange order). Modern ciphers like AES use multiple rounds of both. SubTrans = the foundation of all symmetric ciphers.

keyPoints: A symmetric cipher uses a single, shared secret key for both encryption and decryption.
The model consists of five components: plaintext, encryption algorithm, secret key, ciphertext, and decryption algorithm.
Kerckhoffs's Principle: security depends on key secrecy, not algorithm secrecy.
Two basic operations: substitution (changes element identity) and transposition (changes element position).
The key distribution problem is the fundamental challenge: how to securely share the key before establishing a secure channel.
commonMistakes: Confusing symmetric encryption (one shared key) with asymmetric encryption (public/private key pair).
Believing the algorithm must be kept secret for security, violating Kerckhoffs's Principle.
Confusing brute-force attacks (try all keys) with cryptanalysis (exploit algorithm weaknesses).
Assuming symmetric encryption provides authentication or non-repudiation; it primarily provides confidentiality only.
